topic: Theme 2: Popular Culture (Free-time Activities; Customs, Festivals and Celebrations; Celebrity Culture)
subject: french

items.q: How do you say 'I play the piano' vs 'I play football'?
items.correct: Je joue DU piano (instrument) vs je joue AU football (sport).
items.misconception: Same preposition for both - 'je joue piano', 'je joue football'.
items.random_wrong: Je joue le piano, je joue le football.
items.why: Students drop or confuse prepositions. The rule: jouer DE + instrument (de, du, de la); jouer À + sport/game (à, au, aux). Easy way to lose marks in speaking exams about hobbies.

items.q: How do you say 'I am using my phone right now' (emphasising the action is in progress)?
items.correct: Je suis en train d'utiliser mon portable.
items.why: Students translate English '-ing' as a participle. French has no progressive tense - the present 'j'utilise' covers both 'I use' and 'I am using'. For emphasis on the ongoing action, use 'être en train de + infinitive'.

items.q: What is the French word for 'Christmas'?
items.why: Students omit the diaeresis (tréma). Many festival words have crucial accents: Noël, fête, pâques, élève (student-related). Omitting accents in writing loses marks; the tréma indicates 'e' is pronounced separately.

items.q: Which is the correct way to say 'I play the guitar'?
items.why: It's Je joue de la guitare.. “Je joue à la guitare.” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which sentence means 'I played football yesterday'?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Hier, j'ai joué au foot.. “Hier, je joue au foot.” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many auxiliary verbs are used to form the passé composé in French?
items.why: The real figure is 2 verbs. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
